title: Roast Chicken
subtitle: Buttered breast, lemon and thyme in the cavity, roasted until the skin blisters
excerpt: A properly roasted chicken needs very little: a knob of butter under the breast skin, half a lemon and a few thyme sprigs in the cavity, and a hot oven. The technique is forgiving once you understand where to probe and how long to rest.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: roasting times for fowl. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): resting guidance and cavity aromatics. Traditional British home-cooking practice, cross-referenced across multiple period sources.

content.text: A roast chicken is one of the most reliable meals in the domestic repertoire. A 1.5 kg bird at 200°C (180°C fan) takes around 1 hour 20 minutes from oven door to resting board. The indicators are simple: the juices run clear when you pierce the thigh at its thickest, or an instant-read thermometer shows 75°C at the leg joint.

The small details matter more than elaborate technique. Butter under the breast skin keeps the white meat from drying out. A half lemon and a few thyme sprigs inside the cavity give gentle scent without overwhelming the bird. Resting for 15 minutes is enough for a chicken; it does not need the extended rest of a large joint.
What you need
Method
Butter the breast and season
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Pat the chicken dry with kitchen paper. Using your fingers, ease the skin away from the breast by working from the neck opening down toward the wishbone. Push
{{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter
under the skin and spread it across both breast fillets. Season the skin generously with
{{sea-salt-flakes}}
and
{{black-pepper}}
. Stuff the cavity with the lemon halves, the
{{thyme}} of thyme
, and the garlic cloves if using. Tie the legs together with kitchen twine (
trussing
) if you want a neater shape.
Roast, basting once
Set the chicken breast-up in the roasting pan on the halved onion if using. Roast for 20 minutes until the breast skin begins to colour, then
basting

once with the pan juices. Continue roasting for a further 60 minutes for a 1.5 kg bird. When the juices run clear from the thigh joint, or the thickest part of the leg reads 75°C on a probe, the chicken is done.
Rest and make a simple gravy
Transfer the chicken to a warm plate and rest uncovered for 15 minutes. Tilt the pan and spoon off most of the fat. Set the pan over medium heat, add
{{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock
, and scrape up any fond. Simmer for 3 minutes, season, and strain into a warm jug. Carve at the table.

rows.fix: Pat the skin perfectly dry before buttering. A 10-minute blast at 220°C at the end will crisp pale skin quickly.
rows.cause: Oven not fully preheated, or moisture sitting on the skin surface
rows.symptom: Breast skin is pale after 80 minutes

rows.fix: Return to the oven for 10-minute increments. Cold birds take 15-20 minutes longer. Always probe rather than relying on time alone.
rows.cause: Bird was cold from the fridge, or is larger than expected
rows.symptom: Juices are still pink after the recommended time

rows.fix: Pull the bird when the leg reads 75°C. Carve the breast first and serve it quickly.
rows.cause: Overcooked; breast and legs need different temperatures to finish simultaneously
rows.symptom: Breast meat is dry but legs are perfectly cooked

rows.fix: Always start with a dry bird and a preheated oven. A knob of butter under the breast should prevent sticking.
rows.cause: Not enough fat in the pan, or the pan was cold when the chicken went in

content.text: Where this dish lives

Roast chicken occupies the centre of the British table in a way that few other dishes do. It is Sunday lunch for two, it is the leftover carcass that becomes stock, and it is the cold remains that go into sandwiches on Monday. The bones in the cavity can be simmered into a light broth the same evening. Nothing is wasted.
